cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 28-year-old presents to ED with with persistent vomiting and with type 1 diabetes.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old with chronic respiratory symptoms presents with worsening shortness of breath over several days.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~34.8; measured 34.0 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 22-year-old presents with sudden shortness of breath, tingling fingers, and chest tightness during emotional distress.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.4; measured 21.7 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 59-year-old becomes progressively somnolent with slow respirations after sedation.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~25.7; measured 26.2 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 12.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 66-year-old on haemodialysis presents with malaise and nausea after missing a recent dialysis session.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~35.0 (+/-2); measured 34.6 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 139 - (102 + 18) = 19, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~32.4; measured 32.0 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 29-year-old presents with light-headedness and dehydration after several days of gastroenteritis symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~27.5 (+/-2); measured 25.9 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 139 - (118 + 13) = 8, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 79-year-old on regular frusemide presents with light-headedness and recent poor oral intake after a small increase in diuretic use.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~45.6; measured 45.8 is appropriate. Anion gap is 138 - (96 + 32) = 10, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~26.0 (+/-2); measured 24.3 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 132 - (93 + 12) = 27, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with nausea, hyperventilation, and tinnitus after ingesting a large quantity of tablets.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (28.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 21-year-old presents with tinnitus, vomiting, fever, and rapid breathing after an overdose.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (21.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 66-year-old on haemodialysis presents with malaise and nausea after missing a recent dialysis session.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~35.0 (+/-2); measured 34.5 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 137 - (97 + 18) = 22, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 81-year-old on regular frusemide presents with dizziness, weakness, and poor oral intake after several days of increased diuretic use.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~46.3; measured 46.9 is appropriate. Anion gap is 134 - (92 + 33) = 9, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 67-year-old presents with dizziness and volume depletion after recurrent nausea and retching.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~46.3; measured 47.6 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 19-year-old comes to ED with tachypnoea, dehydration and abdominal pain, and appears volume depleted.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~23.0 (+/-2); measured 22.5 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 133 - (91 + 10) = 32 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: Simple respiratory alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: 35-year-old presents with fast breathing and visible distress after sudden pain.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. For acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~22.0; measured 21.5 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 9.0. This pattern is consistent with simple hyperventilation.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 40-year-old presents with low GCS and hypoventilation after being found collapsed at home.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.8; measured 27.6 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 70-year-old from a nursing home presents with volume depletion following ongoing lower gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~29.0 (+/-2); measured 29.4 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 136 - (110 + 14) = 12, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~20.8; measured 21.2 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.
